tex_content:
\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,geometry,fancyhdr,enumitem,hyperref}
\geometry{margin=1in}
\pagestyle{fancy}
\fancyhf{}
\lhead{ENGR430 - Embedded System Design II}
\rhead{Multiple Choice Quiz}
\cfoot{\thepage}

\title{Multiple Choice Quiz}
\author{ENGR430 - Embedded System Design II}
\date{}

\begin{document}

\maketitle

\section*{Instructions}
Select the best answer for each question. Circle the letter corresponding to the correct answer.

\section*{Questions}

\subsection*{Easy Questions}

\begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{Q\arabic*.}, left=0pt]

\item What function is used to attach an ISR to an interrupt on an Arduino?
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{(\Alph*)}]
    \item attachInterrupt()
    \item setInterrupt()
    \item enableInterrupt()
    \item configureInterrupt()
  \end{enumerate}
  \textbf{Difficulty:} Easy \\
  \textbf{Topic:} Interrupt Handling

\item Which component of a system typically generates an interrupt?
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{(\Alph*)}]
    \item CPU
    \item Peripheral device
    \item Memory
    \item Operating system
  \end{enumerate}
  \textbf{Difficulty:} Easy \\
  \textbf{Topic:} Interrupt Handling

\item What is the purpose of an ISR?
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{(\Alph*)}]
    \item To restart the system
    \item To handle specific interrupt events
    \item To attach interrupts
    \item To disable other interrupts
  \end{enumerate}
  \textbf{Difficulty:} Easy \\
  \textbf{Topic:} Interrupt Handling

\item What is a primary advantage of using interrupts?
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{(\Alph*)}]
    \item Increased CPU load
    \item Immediate processing
    \item Unlimited ISRs
    \item Simpler code
  \end{enumerate}
  \textbf{Difficulty:} Easy \\
  \textbf{Topic:} Interrupt Handling

\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{Medium Questions}

\begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{Q\arabic*.}, left=0pt, resume]

\item What does latency refer to in an interrupt-driven system?
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{(\Alph*)}]
    \item Time taken to execute the ISR
    \item Time delay between an interrupt and ISR execution
    \item Time taken to configure an interrupt
    \item Time taken for the CPU to respond to an interrupt
  \end{enumerate}
  \textbf{Difficulty:} Medium \\
  \textbf{Topic:} Interrupt Performance

\item Which of the following is a technique used to prevent erroneous button presses in an interrupt-driven system?
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{(\Alph*)}]
    \item Polling
    \item Debouncing
    \item Interrupt storming
    \item Latency measurement
  \end{enumerate}
  \textbf{Difficulty:} Medium \\
  \textbf{Topic:} Debouncing Techniques

\item In an interrupt-driven system, how can the performance be optimized?
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{(\Alph*)}]
    \item By increasing ISR execution time
    \item By minimizing the use of delays in ISRs
    \item By using more complex calculations in ISRs
    \item By reducing the number of interrupts
  \end{enumerate}
  \textbf{Difficulty:} Medium \\
  \textbf{Topic:} ISR Optimization

\item What role does an interrupt controller play in a microcontroller system?
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{(\Alph*)}]
    \item It generates interrupts
    \item It manages and prioritizes multiple interrupts
    \item It executes the ISRs
    \item It configures the CPU
  \end{enumerate}
  \textbf{Difficulty:} Medium \\
  \textbf{Topic:} Interrupt Handling

\item What is the best practice for implementing an ISR in Arduino?
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{(\Alph*)}]
    \item Perform lengthy calculations
    \item Keep it as short and efficient as possible
    \item Use delays within the ISR
    \item Call other functions from the ISR
  \end{enumerate}
  \textbf{Difficulty:} Medium \\
  \textbf{Topic:} ISR Implementation

\item What is the main disadvantage of using polling instead of interrupts?
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{(\Alph*)}]
    \item It requires more resources
    \item It is easier to implement
    \item It is faster
    \item It is used in real-time systems
  \end{enumerate}
  \textbf{Difficulty:} Medium \\
  \textbf{Topic:} Polling vs Interrupts

\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{Hard Questions}

\begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{Q\arabic*.}, left=0pt, resume]

\item What is the impact of high latency on a system using interrupts?
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{(\Alph*)}]
    \item Improves system responsiveness
    \item Leads to delayed responses to critical events
    \item Increases CPU usage
    \item Reduces the number of interrupts processed
  \end{enumerate}
  \textbf{Difficulty:} Hard \\
  \textbf{Topic:} Latency Analysis

\item In a system with multiple interrupts, how can priority be effectively managed?
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{(\Alph*)}]
    \item By ignoring lower priority interrupts
    \item By configuring an interrupt controller
    \item By executing all ISRs in the same order
    \item By using a single ISR for all interrupts
  \end{enumerate}
  \textbf{Difficulty:} Hard \\
  \textbf{Topic:} Interrupt Management

\item What is the purpose of measuring ISR execution time?
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{(\Alph*)}]
    \item To increase ISR complexity
    \item To ensure it meets performance requirements
    \item To reduce the number of interrupts
    \item To simplify the code
  \end{enumerate}
  \textbf{Difficulty:} Hard \\
  \textbf{Topic:} Performance Analysis

\item How can debouncing be implemented in an ISR context?
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{(\Alph*)}]
    \item By using hardware components only
    \item By introducing a delay in the ISR
    \item By ignoring the button press
    \item By using a continuous loop in the ISR
  \end{enumerate}
  \textbf{Difficulty:} Hard \\
  \textbf{Topic:} Debouncing Techniques

\end{enumerate}

\newpage

\section*{Answer Key}

\begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{Q\arabic*.}, left=0pt]

\item \textbf{A.} attachInterrupt() \\
  \textbf{Explanation:} The 'attachInterrupt()' function is specifically designed to bind an ISR to a particular interrupt signal on an Arduino, making it the correct choice.

\item \textbf{B.} Peripheral device \\
  \textbf{Explanation:} Peripheral devices, such as sensors or buttons, generate interrupts to signal the CPU that attention is needed, making this the correct answer.

\item \textbf{B.} To handle specific interrupt events \\
  \textbf{Explanation:} An ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) is specifically designed to execute in response to an interrupt, handling the event signaled by the interrupt.

\item \textbf{B.} Immediate processing \\
  \textbf{Explanation:} Interrupts allow immediate processing of events as they occur, enabling responsive system behavior, which is the main advantage over polling.

\item \textbf{B.} Time delay between an interrupt and ISR execution \\
  \textbf{Explanation:} Latency specifically refers to the time delay from the moment an interrupt is triggered to when the corresponding ISR begins execution.

\item \textbf{B.} Debouncing \\
  \textbf{Explanation:} Debouncing is the technique used to filter out noise from mechanical switches, ensuring that only valid signals trigger actions.

\item \textbf{B.} By minimizing the use of delays in ISRs \\
  \textbf{Explanation:} Minimizing the use of delays and ensuring that ISRs are efficient helps to optimize the performance of an interrupt-driven system.

\item \textbf{B.} It manages and prioritizes multiple interrupts \\
  \textbf{Explanation:} An interrupt controller is responsible for managing and prioritizing multiple interrupts in a system, ensuring that the most critical tasks are addressed first.

\item \textbf{B.} Keep it as short and efficient as possible \\
  \textbf{Explanation:} Best practices dictate that ISRs should be kept short and efficient to avoid blocking other processes and to ensure quick responses to interrupts.

\item \textbf{A.} It requires more resources \\
  \textbf{Explanation:} Polling consumes more CPU resources as it repeatedly checks the status of devices, making it less efficient compared to interrupts.

\item \textbf{B.} Leads to delayed responses to critical events \\
  \textbf{Explanation:} High latency can lead to delayed system responses to critical events, which can negatively impact performance and reliability in real-time applications.

\item \textbf{B.} By configuring an interrupt controller \\
  \textbf{Explanation:} An interrupt controller is used to manage and prioritize multiple interrupts, ensuring that higher priority tasks are addressed before lower priority ones.

\item \textbf{B.} To ensure it meets performance requirements \\
  \textbf{Explanation:} Measuring ISR execution time is critical to ensure that it meets performance requirements, particularly in time-sensitive applications.

\item \textbf{B.} By introducing a delay in the ISR \\
  \textbf{Explanation:} Introducing a short delay or checking the input state multiple times can help ensure that only stable button presses are registered, preventing erroneous triggers.

\end{enumerate}

\newpage

assessment:
  mcqs:
    - id: "MCQ1"
      question: "Which of the following best describes the core concept of Introduction to Assembly Language?"
      options:
        A: "Incorrect description A"
        B: "Correct description based on T1"
        C: "Incorrect description C"
        D: "Incorrect description D"
      correct_answer: "B"
      related_outcome: "LO1"
      explanation: "Option B correctly captures the definition from T1. The others confuse related but distinct concepts."
    - id: "MCQ2"
      question: "When applying the method from T2, what is the first step?"
      options:
        A: "The correct first step from T2"
        B: "An incorrect step"
        C: "Another incorrect step"
        D: "A plausible but wrong step"
      correct_answer: "A"
      related_outcome: "LO2"
      explanation: "The correct procedure starts by identifying the problem structure before applying the formula."
    - id: "MCQ3"
      question: "Which condition indicates that the advanced method from T3 is required?"
      options:
        A: "An incorrect condition"
        B: "Another incorrect condition"
        C: "The correct distinguishing condition from T3"
        D: "A plausible but incorrect condition"
      correct_answer: "C"
      related_outcome: "LO3"
      explanation: "The distinguishing condition for using T3 is option C. The basic method is insufficient when this holds."
    - id: "MCQ4"
      question: "Which approach is most efficient for solving a complex problem combining T2 and T3?"
      options:
        A: "An inefficient but valid approach"
        B: "An incorrect approach"
        C: "Another incorrect approach"
        D: "The most efficient approach combining T2 and T3"
      correct_answer: "D"
      related_outcome: "LO4"
      explanation: "Option D leverages the strengths of both methods. The other options are either incorrect or unnecessarily complex."
  short_answer:
    - id: "SA1"
      question: "Define a key term from T1 and explain its significance in Introduction to Assembly Language."
      related_outcome: "LO1"
      model_answer: "A complete answer should define the term precisely, state its significance, and give at least one example."
    - id: "SA2"
      question: "Describe a situation where the advanced method from T3 is necessary and explain why the basic method is insufficient."
      related_outcome: "LO3"
      model_answer: "Students should identify a problem class requiring T3, explain the limitation of T1/T2, and justify the choice."

key_terms:
  - term: "Resistor"
    definition: "A resistor is a passive electrical component that implements electrical resistance as a circuit element. It works by dissipating electrical energy as heat, thereby reducing current flow and dividing voltages within circuits. Resistors are crucial in controlling current and voltage levels, which is essential for biasing active devices and setting gain levels in amplifiers."
    notation: "$R$"
    related_theory: "T2"
  - term: "Capacitor"
    definition: "A capacitor is a passive component that stores energy in an electric field, characterized by its capacitance. It works by accumulating charge on its plates when a voltage is applied, releasing it when needed, thus affecting signal phase and amplitude. Capacitors are vital in tuning and filtering applications, enabling the selection of desired frequencies in RF circuits."
    notation: "$C$"
    related_theory: "T2"
  - term: "Inductor"
    definition: "An inductor is a passive component that stores energy in a magnetic field when electrical current flows through it. It functions by opposing changes in current, a property known as inductive reactance, which is exploited in creating resonant circuits. Inductors are essential in RF design for tuning and filtering, allowing for frequency selection and signal shaping."
    notation: "$L$"
    related_theory: "T3"
  - term: "Diode"
    definition: "A diode is a semiconductor device that allows current to flow in one direction only, based on the p-n junction principle. It operates by permitting charge carriers to move across the junction when forward-biased, blocking them when reverse-biased. Diodes are pivotal in rectification, modulation, and demodulation processes within RF circuits, enabling signal conversion and processing."
    notation: "$I = I_s (e^{\frac{V}{nV_T}} - 1)$"
    related_theory: "T4"
  - term: "Transistor"
    definition: "A transistor is a semiconductor device used to amplify or switch electronic signals and electrical power. It works by controlling the flow of charge carriers through its semiconductor material, with its operation described by models such as the Ebers-Moll. Transistors are fundamental in RF circuits for amplification and switching, crucial for signal processing and power management."
    notation: "N/A"
    related_theory: "T4"
  - term: "Impedance Matching"
    definition: "Impedance matching is the practice of designing the input impedance of an electrical load to maximize power transfer or minimize signal reflection from the source. It involves using components like transformers and transmission lines to adjust circuit impedance. This concept is critical in RF design to ensure efficient power transfer and minimize losses in communication systems."
    notation: "$Z_L = Z_S^*$"
    related_theory: "T5"
  - term: "Ohm's Law"
    definition: "Ohm's Law is a fundamental principle that states the relationship between voltage, current, and resistance in an electrical circuit. It is expressed as $V = IR$, where $V$ is voltage, $I$ is current, and $R$ is resistance. This law is foundational in analyzing and designing circuits, providing a basis for calculating current flow and voltage drops across components."
    notation: "$V = IR$"
    related_theory: "T2"
  - term: "Resonant Circuit"
    definition: "A resonant circuit is an electrical circuit consisting of inductors and capacitors that can oscillate at a specific frequency. It works by storing energy alternately in the electric field of the capacitor and the magnetic field of the inductor. Resonant circuits are crucial in RF applications for selecting and amplifying specific frequencies, essential in filters and oscillators."
    notation: "N/A"
    related_theory: "T3"
  - term: "Quality Factor"
    definition: "The quality factor, or Q factor, is a dimensionless parameter that describes the damping of an oscillator or resonator. It is defined as the ratio of the resonant frequency to the bandwidth over which the circuit can operate effectively. In RF circuits, a high Q factor indicates low energy loss relative to the stored energy, which is critical for applications requiring narrow bandwidths and high selectivity."
    notation: "$Q = \frac{f_0}{BW}$"
    related_theory: "T3"
  - term: "Rectification"
    definition: "Rectification is the process of converting alternating current (AC) to direct current (DC) using diodes. This process is essential in RF circuits for powering devices that require DC voltage. The efficiency and effectiveness of rectification impact the overall performance of RF systems, making it a crucial consideration in power supply design."
    notation: "N/A"
    related_theory: "T4"
  - term: "Bandwidth"
    definition: "Bandwidth refers to the range of frequencies over which a circuit or system can operate effectively. It is determined by the difference between the upper and lower frequency limits of the system. In RF engineering, bandwidth is a critical parameter that affects the data rate and capacity of communication systems, influencing the design and performance of filters and amplifiers."
    notation: "$BW = f_2 - f_1$"
    related_theory: "T3"

theory_sections:
  - id: "T1"
    title: "Introduction to Discrete RF Components"
    content: |
      Discrete RF components form the backbone of modern RF systems, providing essential functions such as amplification, filtering, and signal modulation. These components include resistors, capacitors, inductors, diodes, and transistors, each playing a critical role in circuit design. The working principle of these components is grounded in fundamental electromagnetic theory, where each component manipulates electrical signals in specific ways to achieve desired outcomes.

      For instance, resistors are used to limit current flow and divide voltages, while capacitors store and release energy, affecting the phase and amplitude of signals. Inductors, on the other hand, store energy in a magnetic field when current flows through them, which is crucial for tuning circuits and filtering applications. The mathematical relationships governing these components are described by Ohm's Law for resistors ($V = IR$), the capacitance formula for capacitors ($Q = CV$), and the inductance formula for inductors ($V = L \frac{di}{dt}$).

      In real-world engineering applications, discrete RF components are used in designing RF amplifiers, oscillators, and filters. For example, in a low-noise amplifier (LNA), transistors are employed to amplify weak signals received by an antenna, while capacitors and inductors are used to filter out noise and unwanted frequencies. Understanding the characteristics and limitations of these components is vital for engineers to design efficient and effective RF systems.

    key_points:
      - "Definition and types of discrete RF components"
      - "Basic working principles of resistors, capacitors, and inductors"
      - "Mathematical relationships governing component behavior"
      - "Applications in RF amplifiers, oscillators, and filters"
    related_outcomes:
      - "LO1"
      - "LO2"

  - id: "T2"
    title: "Resistors and Capacitors in RF Circuits"
    content: |
      Resistors and capacitors are fundamental components in RF circuits, each serving distinct purposes. Resistors, characterized by their resistance value measured in ohms, are used to control current flow and divide voltages. They are essential in biasing circuits and setting gain levels in amplifiers. The working principle of a resistor is based on Ohm's Law, which states that the voltage across a resistor is directly proportional to the current flowing through it, expressed as $V = IR$.

      Capacitors, on the other hand, are used to store and release electrical energy, affecting the phase and amplitude of signals. They are characterized by their capacitance value, measured in farads, and are crucial in tuning and filtering applications. The relationship between charge, voltage, and capacitance is given by $Q = CV$. In RF circuits, capacitors are often used in conjunction with inductors to create resonant circuits, which are pivotal in selecting specific frequencies for amplification or attenuation.

      In practical applications, resistors and capacitors are used in RF filters to eliminate unwanted frequencies and in impedance matching networks to ensure maximum power transfer between components. For instance, in a band-pass filter, capacitors and resistors are configured to allow signals within a certain frequency range to pass while attenuating others. This capability is essential in communication systems to isolate desired signals from noise.

    key_points:
      - "Role of resistors in controlling current and voltage"
      - "Capacitors' function in energy storage and signal phase adjustment"
      - "Ohm's Law and capacitance formula"
      - "Applications in RF filters and impedance matching"
    related_outcomes:
      - "LO1"
      - "LO2"

  - id: "T3"
    title: "Inductors and Their Applications in RF Design"
    content: |
      Inductors are passive components that play a crucial role in RF circuit design, primarily used for their ability to store energy in a magnetic field. This property makes them indispensable in tuning and filtering applications. An inductor's behavior is governed by the formula $V = L \frac{di}{dt}$, where $L$ is the inductance measured in henries, $V$ is the voltage across the inductor, and $\frac{di}{dt}$ is the rate of change of current.

      The working principle of an inductor involves the generation of a magnetic field when current flows through its coil. This magnetic field can induce a voltage that opposes changes in current, a phenomenon known as inductive reactance. This property is exploited in RF circuits to create resonant circuits when combined with capacitors, allowing for the selection and amplification of specific frequencies.

      In real-world applications, inductors are used in RF amplifiers, oscillators, and filters. For example, in a radio transmitter, inductors are part of the tank circuit that determines the frequency of oscillation. They are also used in RF chokes to block high-frequency AC signals while allowing DC to pass. Understanding the characteristics of inductors, such as their quality factor and self-resonant frequency, is essential for designing efficient RF systems.

    key_points:
      - "Inductors' role in energy storage and magnetic field generation"
      - "Inductive reactance and its impact on current flow"
      - "Mathematical expression for inductors"
      - "Applications in RF amplifiers, oscillators, and chokes"
    related_outcomes:
      - "LO1"
      - "LO3"

  - id: "T4"
    title: "Diodes and Transistors in RF Circuits"
    content: |
      Diodes and transistors are active components that are integral to RF circuit design. Diodes, known for their ability to allow current to flow in one direction, are used in rectification, signal modulation, and demodulation. The working principle of a diode is based on the p-n junction, where the flow of charge carriers is controlled by the potential barrier. The current-voltage relationship of a diode is given by the Shockley diode equation: $I = I_s (e^{\frac{V}{nV_T}} - 1)$, where $I_s$ is the saturation current, $V$ is the voltage across the diode, $n$ is the ideality factor, and $V_T$ is the thermal voltage.

      Transistors, on the other hand, are used for amplification and switching purposes. They operate by controlling the flow of charge carriers through a semiconductor material, with the current gain determined by the transistor's configuration (e.g., common emitter, common base). The basic operation of a bipolar junction transistor (BJT) is described by the Ebers-Moll model, which provides the relationship between the base, collector, and emitter currents.

      In RF applications, diodes are used in mixers and detectors, while transistors are employed in amplifiers and oscillators. For example, in an RF amplifier, a transistor is used to increase the power of a signal, while diodes in a mixer combine two frequencies to produce new frequencies. Understanding the characteristics and limitations of these components is crucial for designing robust RF systems.

    key_points:
      - "Diodes' function in rectification and modulation"
      - "Transistors' role in amplification and switching"
      - "Shockley diode equation and Ebers-Moll model"
      - "Applications in mixers, detectors, and amplifiers"
    related_outcomes:
      - "LO2"
      - "LO3"

  - id: "T5"
    title: "Impedance Matching in RF Circuits"
    content: |
      Impedance matching is a critical concept in RF circuit design, ensuring maximum power transfer between components and minimizing signal reflection. The principle of impedance matching is based on the concept that the load impedance ($Z_L$) should be the complex conjugate of the source impedance ($Z_S$) to achieve maximum power transfer. This is mathematically expressed as $Z_L = Z_S^*$.

      The working principle involves using components such as transformers, capacitors, and inductors to adjust the impedance of a circuit. For instance, a quarter-wave transformer is a transmission line section used to match impedances by transforming the load impedance to the desired value. The characteristic impedance of the transformer is chosen to satisfy the matching condition.

      In practical applications, impedance matching is crucial in RF amplifiers, antennas, and transmission lines. For example, in an RF amplifier, impedance matching networks are used to ensure that the amplifier's output impedance matches the load impedance, maximizing power transfer and minimizing distortion. Understanding the techniques and tools for impedance matching is essential for RF engineers to design efficient and effective systems.

    key_points:
      - "Importance of impedance matching in RF circuits"
      - "Principle of maximum power transfer"
      - "Use of transformers and transmission lines for matching"
      - "Applications in amplifiers and antennas"
    related_outcomes:
      - "LO2"
      - "LO4"

  - id: "T6"
    title: "Advanced RF Circuit Design Techniques"
    content: |
      Advanced RF circuit design techniques involve the integration of various components and technologies to achieve specific performance criteria. These techniques include the use of surface mount technology (SMT), microstrip lines, and advanced simulation tools to optimize circuit performance. The working principle of these techniques is grounded in the precise control of component placement and interconnections to minimize parasitic effects and enhance signal integrity.

      Mathematical modeling and simulation play a crucial role in advanced RF design, allowing engineers to predict circuit behavior and optimize parameters before physical implementation. Tools such as SPICE and electromagnetic simulation software are used to model complex interactions between components and the electromagnetic environment.

      In real-world applications, advanced RF design techniques are employed in the development of high-frequency communication systems, radar, and satellite technology. For example, microstrip lines are used in RF filters and antennas to achieve compact and efficient designs. Understanding these techniques and their applications is essential for RF engineers to innovate and push the boundaries of technology.

    key_points:
      - "Integration of components using SMT and microstrip lines"
      - "Role of simulation in RF circuit optimization"
      - "Use of advanced tools like SPICE and EM simulators"
      - "Applications in communication systems and radar"
    related_outcomes:
      - "LO3"
      - "LO5"

worked_examples:
  - id: "EX1"
    title: "Calculating Impedance in a Series RLC Circuit"
    difficulty_level: "basic"
    related_theory: "T2"
    problem: "Determine the total impedance of a series RLC circuit with a resistor of 50 Ω, an inductor of 100 μH, and a capacitor of 10 pF at a frequency of 1 MHz."
    given_information:
      - "Resistance, $R = 50 \, \Omega$"
      - "Inductance, $L = 100 \, \mu H$"
      - "Capacitance, $C = 10 \, pF$"
      - "Frequency, $f = 1 \, MHz$"
    required: "Find the total impedance $Z$ of the circuit."
    solution_steps:
      - step: 1
        explanation: "Calculate the inductive reactance $X_L$ using $X_L = 2\pi fL$."
        calculation: "$X_L = 2\pi \times 1 \times 10^6 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 628.32 \, \Omega$"
      - step: 2
        explanation: "Calculate the capacitive reactance $X_C$ using $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi fC}$."
        calculation: "$X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1 \times 10^6 \times 10 \times 10^{-12}} = 15915.49 \, \Omega$"
      - step: 3
        explanation: "Determine the total reactance $X = X_L - X_C$."
        calculation: "$X = 628.32 - 15915.49 = -15287.17 \, \Omega$"
      - step: 4
        explanation: "Calculate the total impedance $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$."
        calculation: "$Z = \sqrt{50^2 + (-15287.17)^2} = 15287.18 \, \Omega$"
      - step: 5
        explanation: "Express the impedance in polar form."
        calculation: "$Z = 15287.18 \angle -89.81^\circ \, \Omega$"
    final_answer: "$\boxed{15287.18 \angle -89.81^\circ \, \Omega}$"
    teaching_notes: "This example illustrates the calculation of impedance in a series RLC circuit, emphasizing the importance of understanding reactance and impedance in AC circuits."
    common_mistakes:
      - "Forgetting to convert units correctly, especially for frequency and capacitance."
      - "Incorrectly calculating the difference between inductive and capacitive reactance."

  - id: "EX2"
    title: "Designing a Low-Pass Filter with a Cutoff Frequency"
    difficulty_level: "intermediate"
    related_theory: "T2"
    problem: "Design a low-pass RC filter with a cutoff frequency of 10 kHz using a 1 kΩ resistor."
    given_information:
      - "Resistance, $R = 1 \, k\Omega$"
      - "Cutoff frequency, $f_c = 10 \, kHz$"
    required: "Find the required capacitance $C$."
    solution_steps:
      - step: 1
        explanation: "Use the cutoff frequency formula for an RC filter: $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$."
        calculation: "$10 \times 10^3 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1 \times 10^3 \times C}$"
      - step: 2
        explanation: "Rearrange the formula to solve for $C$."
        calculation: "$C = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^3}$"
      - step: 3
        explanation: "Calculate the capacitance."
        calculation: "$C = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^7} = 15.92 \, nF$"
      - step: 4
        explanation: "Verify the calculation by substituting back into the cutoff frequency formula."
        calculation: "$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1 \times 10^3 \times 15.92 \times 10^{-9}} = 10 \, kHz$"
      - step: 5
        explanation: "Confirm the design meets the specifications."
        calculation: "The calculated capacitance ensures the desired cutoff frequency."
    final_answer: "$\boxed{15.92 \, nF}$"
    teaching_notes: "This example demonstrates the design of a basic RC filter, highlighting the relationship between resistance, capacitance, and cutoff frequency."
    common_mistakes:
      - "Misapplying the cutoff frequency formula."
      - "Neglecting to verify the design by substituting back into the formula."

  - id: "EX3"
    title: "Analyzing a Parallel LC Resonant Circuit"
    difficulty_level: "advanced"
    related_theory: "T3"
    problem: "Analyze a parallel LC circuit with an inductor of 10 μH and a capacitor of 100 pF. Determine the resonant frequency."
    given_information:
      - "Inductance, $L = 10 \, \mu H$"
      - "Capacitance, $C = 100 \, pF$"
    required: "Find the resonant frequency $f_0$."
    solution_steps:
      - step: 1
        explanation: "Use the resonant frequency formula for an LC circuit: $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$."
        calculation: "$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{-12}}}$"
      - step: 2
        explanation: "Calculate the product $LC$."
        calculation: "$LC = 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{-12} = 10^{-15}$"
      - step: 3
        explanation: "Substitute $LC$ into the resonant frequency formula."
        calculation: "$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{10^{-15}}}$"
      - step: 4
        explanation: "Compute the resonant frequency."
        calculation: "$f_0 = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-8}} = 15.92 \, MHz$"
      - step: 5
        explanation: "Verify the result by considering the implications on circuit behavior."
        calculation: "At $15.92 \, MHz$, the circuit will have maximum impedance."
    final_answer: "$\boxed{15.92 \, MHz}$"
    teaching_notes: "This example emphasizes the concept of resonance in LC circuits and the importance of precise calculations in determining resonant frequency."
    common_mistakes:
      - "Incorrectly calculating the square root of the product $LC$."
      - "Forgetting to convert units appropriately for frequency calculations."

  - id: "EX4"
    title: "Determining the Quality Factor of an RLC Circuit"
    difficulty_level: "challenge"
    related_theory: "T3"
    problem: "Calculate the quality factor of a series RLC circuit with $R = 10 \, \Omega$, $L = 1 \, mH$, and $C = 1 \, \mu F$ at its resonant frequency."
    given_information:
      - "Resistance, $R = 10 \, \Omega$"
      - "Inductance, $L = 1 \, mH$"
      - "Capacitance, $C = 1 \, \mu F$"
    required: "Find the quality factor $Q$."
    solution_steps:
      - step: 1
        explanation: "Determine the resonant frequency $f_0$ using $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$."
        calculation: "$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{1 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}} = 159.15 \, kHz$"
      - step: 2
        explanation: "Use the formula for the quality factor $Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$."
        calculation: "$Q = \frac{1}{10}\sqrt{\frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}}}$"
      - step: 3
        explanation: "Calculate the ratio $\frac{L}{C}$."
        calculation: "$\frac{L}{C} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 1000$"
      - step: 4
        explanation: "Substitute the ratio into the quality factor formula."
        calculation: "$Q = \frac{1}{10} \times \sqrt{1000} = 3.16$"
      - step: 5
        explanation: "Interpret the quality factor in terms of circuit selectivity."
        calculation: "A $Q$ of 3.16 indicates moderate selectivity."
    final_answer: "$\boxed{3.16}$"
    teaching_notes: "This example illustrates the calculation of the quality factor, highlighting its significance in determining the selectivity and bandwidth of an RLC circuit."
    common_mistakes:
      - "Misapplying the quality factor formula."
      - "Incorrectly computing the square root of the ratio $\frac{L}{C}$."

  - id: "EX5"
    title: "Calculating Power Dissipation in a Resistor"
    difficulty_level: "basic"
    related_theory: "T2"
    problem: "Determine the power dissipated by a 100 Ω resistor when a current of 0.5 A flows through it."
    given_information:
      - "Resistance, $R = 100 \, \Omega$"
      - "Current, $I = 0.5 \, A$"
    required: "Find the power $P$ dissipated by the resistor."
    solution_steps:
      - step: 1
        explanation: "Use the power formula $P = I^2R$."
        calculation: "$P = (0.5)^2 \times 100$"
      - step: 2
        explanation: "Calculate the square of the current."
        calculation: "$0.5^2 = 0.25$"
      - step: 3
        explanation: "Substitute the squared current into the power formula."
        calculation: "$P = 0.25 \times 100 = 25 \, W$"
      - step: 4
        explanation: "Verify the calculation by considering Ohm's Law."
        calculation: "Using $V = IR$, $V = 0.5 \times 100 = 50 \, V$"
      - step: 5
        explanation: "Recalculate power using $P = VI$ to confirm."
        calculation: "$P = 50 \times 0.5 = 25 \, W$"
    final_answer: "$\boxed{25 \, W}$"
    teaching_notes: "This example reinforces the use of power formulas and the verification of results through multiple methods."
    common_mistakes:
      - "Incorrectly applying the power formula."
      - "Forgetting to verify calculations with an alternative method."

    related_outcome: "LO4"
    suggested_answer: "The preferred approach and justification."

practice_problems:
  - id: "PP1"
    problem: "A series RLC circuit consists of a 50 Ω resistor, a 100 μH inductor, and a 10 pF capacitor. Calculate the total impedance at a frequency of 1 MHz."
    difficulty: "basic"
    related_example: "EX1"
    hints:
      - "Calculate the inductive and capacitive reactance first."
      - "Use the formula $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$ to find the impedance."
    expected_answer: "15287.18 Ω"

  - id: "PP2"
    problem: "Design a low-pass filter with a cutoff frequency of 5 kHz using a 2 kΩ resistor. Determine the required capacitance."
    difficulty: "basic"
    related_example: "EX2"
    hints:
      - "Use the formula $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ to find the capacitance."
      - "Rearrange the formula to solve for $C$."
    expected_answer: "15.92 nF"

  - id: "PP3"
    problem: "A parallel LC circuit has an inductor of 10 μH and a capacitor of 100 pF. What is the resonant frequency of this circuit?"
    difficulty: "intermediate"
    related_example: "EX3"
    hints:
      - "Use the formula $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$."
      - "Calculate the product $LC$ before finding the resonant frequency."
    expected_answer: "15.92 MHz"

  - id: "PP4"
    problem: "A series RLC circuit with $R = 10 \, \Omega$, $L = 1 \, mH$, and $C = 1 \, \mu F$ is at its resonant frequency. Calculate the quality factor."
    difficulty: "advanced"
    related_example: "EX4"
    hints:
      - "Determine the resonant frequency first."
      - "Use the formula $Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$."
    expected_answer: "3.16"

  - id: "PP5"
    problem: "A 100 Ω resistor dissipates power when a current of 0.5 A flows through it. Calculate the power dissipation."
    difficulty: "basic"
    related_example: "EX5"
    hints:
      - "Use the power formula $P = I^2R$."
      - "Verify your result using $P = VI$."
    expected_answer: "25 W"

  - id: "PP6"
    problem: "An RF circuit requires impedance matching. If the source impedance is 50 Ω, what should the load impedance be for maximum power transfer?"
    difficulty: "intermediate"
    related_example: "EX1"
    hints:
      - "Recall the principle of impedance matching: $Z_L = Z_S^*$."
      - "Consider the complex conjugate of the source impedance."
    expected_answer: "50 Ω"

  - id: "PP7"
    problem: "A 200 μH inductor and a 50 pF capacitor form a parallel LC circuit. Calculate the resonant frequency."
    difficulty: "intermediate"
    related_example: "EX3"
    hints:
      - "Use the formula $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$."
      - "Ensure unit conversions are correct before calculating."
    expected_answer: "1.59 MHz"

  - id: "PP8"
    problem: "Design a high-pass filter with a cutoff frequency of 20 kHz using a 500 Ω resistor. Determine the required capacitance."
    difficulty: "intermediate"
    related_example: "EX2"
    hints:
      - "Use the formula $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ to find the capacitance."
      - "Rearrange the formula to solve for $C$."
    expected_answer: "15.92 nF"

  - id: "PP9"
    problem: "A series RLC circuit is tuned to a frequency of 1 MHz. If the circuit has a resistance of 20 Ω, an inductance of 50 μH, and a capacitance of 80 pF, calculate the quality factor."
    difficulty: "challenge"
    related_example: "EX4"
    hints:
      - "First, calculate the resonant frequency."
      - "Use the formula $Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$."
    expected_answer: "7.07"

  - id: "PP10"
    problem: "A 75 Ω resistor is connected in parallel with a 150 Ω resistor. Calculate the equivalent resistance."
    difficulty: "basic"
    related_example: "EX1"
    hints:
      - "Use the formula for parallel resistors: $\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}$."
      - "Calculate the reciprocal of the sum to find $R_{eq}$."
    expected_answer: "50 Ω"

assessment:
  mcqs:
    - id: "MCQ1"
      question: "Which component is primarily used to store energy in a magnetic field in RF circuits?"
      options:
        A: "Resistor"
        B: "Capacitor"
        C: "Inductor (correct)"
        D: "Diode"
      correct_answer: "C"
      related_outcome: "LO1"
      explanation: "Inductors store energy in a magnetic field, which is essential for tuning and filtering applications in RF circuits."
    - id: "MCQ2"
      question: "What is the primary function of a capacitor in RF circuits?"
      options:
        A: "To store energy in a magnetic field"
        B: "To allow current flow in one direction"
        C: "To store energy in an electric field (correct)"
        D: "To amplify signals"
      correct_answer: "C"
      related_outcome: "LO1"
      explanation: "Capacitors store energy in an electric field and are crucial for tuning and filtering applications."
    - id: "MCQ3"
      question: "Which equation represents Ohm's Law?"
      options:
        A: "$V = IR$ (correct)"
        B: "$Q = CV$"
        C: "$I = I_s (e^{\frac{V}{nV_T}} - 1)$"
        D: "$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$"
      correct_answer: "A"
      related_outcome: "LO1"
      explanation: "Ohm's Law is expressed as $V = IR$, relating voltage, current, and resistance."
    - id: "MCQ4"
      question: "In a series RLC circuit, what happens to the total impedance at resonance?"
      options:
        A: "It becomes zero"
        B: "It reaches a maximum value"
        C: "It equals the resistance (correct)"
        D: "It becomes infinite"
      correct_answer: "C"
      related_outcome: "LO3"
      explanation: "At resonance, the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel each other out, leaving only the resistive component."
    - id: "MCQ5"
      question: "Which component is used for rectification in RF circuits?"
      options:
        A: "Transistor"
        B: "Inductor"
        C: "Diode (correct)"
        D: "Capacitor"
      correct_answer: "C"
      related_outcome: "LO2"
      explanation: "Diodes allow current to flow in one direction and are used for rectification in RF circuits."
    - id: "MCQ6"
      question: "What is the purpose of impedance matching in RF circuits?"
      options:
        A: "To increase signal frequency"
        B: "To minimize power transfer"
        C: "To maximize power transfer (correct)"
        D: "To reduce circuit complexity"
      correct_answer: "C"
      related_outcome: "LO4"
      explanation: "Impedance matching ensures maximum power transfer between components by matching the load impedance to the source impedance."
  short_answer:
    - id: "SA1"
      question: "Explain the role of inductors in RF circuit design."
      related_outcome: "LO1"
      model_answer: "Inductors store energy in a magnetic field, which is crucial for tuning and filtering applications. They help in selecting and amplifying specific frequencies by creating resonant circuits when combined with capacitors."
    - id: "SA2"
      question: "Describe how impedance matching is achieved in RF circuits."
      related_outcome: "LO4"
      model_answer: "Impedance matching is achieved by adjusting the load impedance to be the complex conjugate of the source impedance, often using components like transformers and transmission lines, to ensure maximum power transfer and minimize signal reflection."

tex_content:
\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,geometry,fancyhdr,enumitem,hyperref}
\geometry{a4paper, margin=1in}
\pagestyle{fancy}
\fancyhf{}
\rhead{ENGR428 - RF, Antennas and Microwave Circuits}
\lhead{Multiple Choice Quiz}
\cfoot{\thepage}

\title{ENGR428 - RF, Antennas and Microwave Circuits\\Multiple Choice Quiz}
\date{}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\section*{Instructions}
Select the best answer for each question. Indicate your choice clearly.

\section*{Questions}

\subsection*{Easy Questions}

\begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{Q\arabic*.}, leftmargin=0pt, itemindent=*, itemsep=1em]

\item Which component is primarily used to store energy in a magnetic field in RF circuits?\\
\textbf{Topic:} Inductors\\
\textbf{Difficulty:} Easy
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\Alph*)]
    \item Resistor
    \item Capacitor
    \item Inductor
    \item Diode
\end{enumerate}

\item What is the primary function of a capacitor in RF circuits?\\
\textbf{Topic:} Capacitors\\
\textbf{Difficulty:} Easy
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\Alph*)]
    \item To store energy in a magnetic field
    \item To allow current flow in one direction
    \item To store energy in an electric field
    \item To amplify signals
\end{enumerate}

\item Which equation represents Ohm's Law?\\
\textbf{Topic:} Ohm's Law\\
\textbf{Difficulty:} Easy
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\Alph*)]
    \item $V = IR$
    \item $Q = CV$
    \item $I = I_s \left(e^{\frac{V}{nV_T}} - 1\right)$
    \item $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$
\end{enumerate}

\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{Medium Questions}

\begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{Q\arabic*.}, leftmargin=0pt, itemindent=*, itemsep=1em, start=4]

\item In a series RLC circuit, what happens to the total impedance at resonance?\\
\textbf{Topic:} Impedance in RLC circuits\\
\textbf{Difficulty:} Medium
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\Alph*)]
    \item It becomes zero
    \item It reaches a maximum value
    \item It equals the resistance
    \item It becomes infinite
\end{enumerate}

\item Which component is used for rectification in RF circuits?\\
\textbf{Topic:} Diodes\\
\textbf{Difficulty:} Medium
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\Alph*)]
    \item Transistor
    \item Inductor
    \item Diode
    \item Capacitor
\end{enumerate}

\item What is the purpose of impedance matching in RF circuits?\\
\textbf{Topic:} Impedance Matching\\
\textbf{Difficulty:} Medium
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\Alph*)]
    \item To increase signal frequency
    \item To minimize power transfer
    \item To maximize power transfer
    \item To reduce circuit complexity
\end{enumerate}

\item A parallel LC circuit has an inductor of 10 \si{\micro\henry} and a capacitor of 100 \si{\pico\farad}. What is the resonant frequency of this circuit?\\
\textbf{Topic:} Resonant Frequency\\
\textbf{Difficulty:} Medium
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\Alph*)]
    \item 1.59 \si{\mega\hertz}
    \item 15.92 \si{\mega\hertz}
    \item 100 \si{\kilo\hertz}
    \item 10 \si{\kilo\hertz}
\end{enumerate}

\item What is the role of diodes in RF circuit design?\\
\textbf{Topic:} Diodes\\
\textbf{Difficulty:} Medium
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\Alph*)]
    \item To amplify signals
    \item To allow current flow in one direction
    \item To store energy in a magnetic field
    \item To match impedances
\end{enumerate}

\item In RF circuits, what does the term 'Quality Factor' (Q) indicate?\\
\textbf{Topic:} Quality Factor\\
\textbf{Difficulty:} Medium
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\Alph*)]
    \item The frequency of oscillation
    \item The damping of the oscillator
    \item The power transfer efficiency
    \item The impedance level
\end{enumerate}

\item How does the principle of impedance matching affect RF circuit performance?\\
\textbf{Topic:} Impedance Matching\\
\textbf{Difficulty:} Medium
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\Alph*)]
    \item It increases signal distortion
    \item It minimizes signal reflection
    \item It reduces the bandwidth
    \item It simplifies circuit complexity
\end{enumerate}

\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{Hard Questions}

\begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{Q\arabic*.}, leftmargin=0pt, itemindent=*, itemsep=1em, start=11]

\item Calculate the total impedance of a series RLC circuit with a resistor of 50 \si{\ohm}, an inductor of 100 \si{\micro\henry}, and a capacitor of 10 \si{\pico\farad} at a frequency of 1 \si{\mega\hertz}. What is the correct impedance?\\
\textbf{Topic:} Impedance Calculation\\
\textbf{Difficulty:} Hard
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\Alph*)]
    \item 15287.18 \si{\ohm}
    \item 100 \si{\ohm}
    \item 50 \si{\ohm}
    \item 200 \si{\ohm}
\end{enumerate}

\item Design a low-pass RC filter with a cutoff frequency of 10 \si{\kilo\hertz} using a 1 \si{\kilo\ohm} resistor. What is the required capacitance?\\
\textbf{Topic:} Filter Design\\
\textbf{Difficulty:} Hard
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\Alph*)]
    \item 15.92 \si{\nano\farad}
    \item 1 \si{\nano\farad}
    \item 100 \si{\nano\farad}
    \item 10 \si{\nano\farad}
\end{enumerate}

\item Calculate the quality factor of a series RLC circuit with $R = 10$ \si{\ohm}, $L = 1$ \si{\milli\henry}, and $C = 1$ \si{\micro\farad} at its resonant frequency. What is the quality factor?\\
\textbf{Topic:} Quality Factor\\
\textbf{Difficulty:} Hard
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\Alph*)]
    \item 3.16
    \item 5
    \item 10
    \item 1.5
\end{enumerate}

\end{enumerate}

\newpage
\section*{Answer Key}

\begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{Q\arabic*.}, leftmargin=0pt, itemindent=*, itemsep=1em]

\item C. Inductor\\
\textbf{Explanation:} Inductors store energy in a magnetic field, which is essential for tuning and filtering applications in RF circuits.

\item C. To store energy in an electric field\\
\textbf{Explanation:} Capacitors store energy in an electric field and are crucial for tuning and filtering applications.

\item A. $V = IR$\\
\textbf{Explanation:} Ohm's Law is expressed as $V = IR$, relating voltage, current, and resistance.

\item C. It equals the resistance\\
\textbf{Explanation:} At resonance, the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel each other out, leaving only the resistive component.

\item C. Diode\\
\textbf{Explanation:} Diodes allow current to flow in one direction and are used for rectification in RF circuits.

\item C. To maximize power transfer\\
\textbf{Explanation:} Impedance matching ensures maximum power transfer between components by matching the load impedance to the source impedance.

\item A. 1.59 \si{\mega\hertz}\\
\textbf{Explanation:} The resonant frequency is calculated using the LC formula, yielding 1.59 \si{\mega\hertz}.

\item B. To allow current flow in one direction\\
\textbf{Explanation:} Diodes are used for rectification and allow current to flow in one direction, which is essential in RF applications.

\item B. The damping of the oscillator\\
\textbf{Explanation:} The Quality Factor indicates how underdamped an oscillator or resonator is, reflecting its bandwidth relative to its center frequency.

\item B. It minimizes signal reflection\\
\textbf{Explanation:} Impedance matching ensures maximum power transfer and minimizes signal reflection, improving circuit efficiency.

\item A. 15287.18 \si{\ohm}\\
\textbf{Explanation:} The total impedance combines resistance and reactance, calculated from the given values leading to 15287.18 \si{\ohm}.

\item A. 15.92 \si{\nano\farad}\\
\textbf{Explanation:} The required capacitance is calculated using the cutoff frequency formula for an RC filter, resulting in 15.92 \si{\nano\farad}.

\item A. 3.16\\
\textbf{Explanation:} The quality factor is derived from the relationship between resistance, inductance, and capacitance, resulting in 3.16.

\end{enumerate}

\newpage
\section*{Score Tracking}

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Question} \& \textbf{Your Answer} \& \textbf{Correct Answer} \\
\hline
1 \& \& C \\
2 \& \& C \\
3 \& \& A \\
4 \& \& C \\
5 \& \& C \\
6 \& \& C \\
7 \& \& A \\
8 \& \& B \\
9 \& \& B \\
10 \& \& B \\
11 \& \& A \\
12 \& \& A \\
13 \& \& A \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\end{document}

subject: Timer-Based Proximity Monitoring System

yaml_content:
metadata:
  lesson_id: ENGR430W13L2
  created: 2026-03-16
  author: Curriculum Designer AI
  schema_version: "3.0"
course:
  code: ENGR430
  title: Embedded System Design II
  subject: Timer-Based Proximity Monitoring System
lesson:
  week: 13
  number: 2
  topic: Timer-Based Proximity Monitoring System
  date: 2026-03-16
  duration_minutes: 75
  lesson_type: lecture
constraints:
  max_pages: 20
  include_diagrams: true
  required_worked_examples: 4
  required_learning_outcomes: 4
  required_key_terms: 8
  required_practice_problems: 8
  include_homework: true
  include_mcq: true
learning_outcomes:
  - id: LO1
    description: Understand the principles and operation of timer-based systems in embedded applications, including their role in proximity monitoring.
    bloom_level: understand
    clo_mapping: CLO2
    assessment_method: Quiz and short-answer questions
  - id: LO2
    description: Apply mathematical models and formulas to design and implement timer-based proximity monitoring systems.
    bloom_level: apply
    clo_mapping: CLO4
    assessment_method: Design project and lab exercises
  - id: LO3
    description: Analyze various proximity monitoring techniques using timers, considering factors such as accuracy and response time.
    bloom_level: analyze
    clo_mapping: CLO5
    assessment_method: Case study analysis and lab reports
  - id: LO4
    description: Evaluate the effectiveness and efficiency of different timer-based designs for proximity systems in real-world applications.
    bloom_level: evaluate
    clo_mapping: CLO6
    assessment_method: Peer review and project presentations
  - id: LO5
    description: Create and simulate a timer-based proximity monitoring system using software tools, demonstrating the integration of hardware and software components.
    bloom_level: create
    clo_mapping: CLO7
    assessment_method: Simulation project and software demonstration
prerequisites:
  - topic: Basic Timer Circuits
    description: Familiarity with basic timer circuits including the understanding of oscillation and the role of capacitors and resistors in timing circuits.
  - topic: Embedded System Fundamentals
    description: Knowledge of embedded systems architecture, including microcontrollers and interfacing techniques.
  - topic: Signal Processing Basics
    description: Understanding of signal processing fundamentals, particularly filtering and noise reduction techniques.
key_terms:
  - term: Timer Interrupts
    definition: Timer interrupts are signals emitted by hardware timers in microcontrollers to manage the execution of specific tasks at set intervals. They enable the precise scheduling of tasks, which is essential for maintaining the timing accuracy required in real-time systems. In engineering, they are crucial for applications that demand consistent timing, such as in embedded systems where precise task execution is mandatory.
    notation: \text{Interrupt}
    related_theory: T1
  - term: Duty Cycle
    definition: The duty cycle is the fraction of one period in which a signal or system is active, typically expressed as a percentage. It plays a significant role in pulse-width modulation (PWM), which is used to control the power delivered to electrical devices. Understanding the duty cycle is vital in engineering for designing systems that require precise control over power and signal modulation.
    notation: \text{DutyCycle} = \frac{\text{ActiveTime}}{\text{TotalPeriod}}
    related_theory: T2
  - term: Proximity Sensors
    definition: Proximity sensors are devices that detect the presence or absence of an object within a defined range using technologies such as infrared, ultrasonic, or capacitive sensing. They are integral to automation and safety systems, providing non-contact detection capabilities. In engineering, these sensors are essential for creating systems that require object detection and distance measurement without physical contact.
    notation: \text{Sensor}
    related_theory: T3
  - term: Latency
    definition: Latency refers to the time delay between the initiation of a process and its execution, a critical factor in systems requiring real-time processing. High latency can degrade system performance, making it essential to minimize in applications like proximity monitoring systems. In engineering, understanding and managing latency is crucial for optimizing the performance and responsiveness of embedded systems.
    notation: \text{Latency}
    related_theory: T1
  - term: Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)
    definition: A Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) is designed to manage hardware resources and run applications with precise timing and high reliability. It is essential for deploying real-time embedded systems where tasks must be executed within strict timing constraints. In engineering, RTOSs are fundamental for applications that require deterministic behavior and high reliability, such as in industrial automation and robotics.
    notation: \text{RTOS}
    related_theory: T4
  - term: Feedback Loop
    definition: A feedback loop is a system structure where the output is fed back into the input to maintain a desired state, often used in control systems to ensure stability and accuracy. Feedback loops are critical in engineering for designing systems that require precise control and adaptation to changing conditions. They are widely used in applications such as temperature regulation, motor control, and automated systems.
    notation: \text{FeedbackLoop}
    related_theory: T3
  - term: Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM)
    definition: Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM) is a technique used to encode a message into a pulsing signal by varying the width of the pulses. It is extensively used in controlling the power supplied to electrical devices, especially in motor control and LED dimming. In engineering, PWM is vital for applications that require efficient power management and control, providing a method to simulate analog control using digital signals.
    notation: \text{PWM}
    related_theory: T2
  - term: Signal Conditioning
    definition: Signal conditioning involves manipulating an analog signal in such a way that it meets the requirements of the next stage for further processing. This process includes amplification, filtering, and conversion to digital form. In engineering, signal conditioning is crucial for ensuring that sensor outputs are accurately interpreted by microcontrollers and other digital systems, thereby enhancing the reliability and accuracy of data acquisition systems.
    notation: \text{SignalConditioning}
    related_theory: T3
theory_sections:
  - id: T1
    title: Introduction to Timer-Based Systems
    content: The use of timers in embedded systems is fundamental for applications requiring precise timekeeping and control. Timers can be hardware-based, integrated into microcontrollers, or software-based, relying on program execution timing. They serve multiple purposes, such as generating time delays, measuring intervals, and triggering events. The key to leveraging timers effectively lies in understanding their configuration and the role of interrupts in maintaining system responsiveness. In real-time systems, timers are essential for managing processes like task scheduling, input/output operations, and communication protocols. By employing timers, systems can operate autonomously and react to changes instantly without continuous CPU intervention. The mathematical modeling of timer operations involves calculations of frequency, period, and duty cycle, which are crucial for designing systems that meet specific timing requirements. For instance, in a timer with a frequency of $f = 1/T$, where $T$ is the period, the configuration of the timer registers will determine the behavior of the system under various conditions. In practical applications, such as in automotive systems, timers are used to control the timing of fuel injection and ignition systems, ensuring optimal engine performance.
    key_points:
      - Importance of timers in embedded systems
      - Types of timers and their configurations
      - Role of interrupts in timing operations
      - Mathematical modeling of timer functions
      - Applications in real-time systems
    related_outcomes: [LO1, LO2]
  - id: T2
    title: Proximity Monitoring Techniques
    content: Proximity monitoring systems utilize sensors to detect the presence or absence of objects within a defined range. These systems can employ various sensing technologies, including infrared, ultrasonic, and capacitive sensing. Each method has its advantages and limitations; for example, infrared sensors offer simplicity and low cost but may be affected by environmental conditions, while ultrasonic sensors provide accuracy over longer distances. The design of a proximity monitoring system involves selecting the appropriate sensor technology and integrating it with a timer-based control mechanism to ensure timely response and efficient power management. The use of pulse-width modulation (PWM) and duty cycle adjustments allows for dynamic control of sensor operation, optimizing performance while conserving energy. The integration of these systems into a broader control loop can enhance capabilities such as object tracking and collision avoidance, which are vital in industrial automation and robotics. In automotive parking systems, proximity sensors help in detecting obstacles, thereby preventing collisions and enhancing safety.
    key_points:
      - Overview of proximity sensors and technologies
      - Integration with timer-based systems
      - Advantages and limitations of different sensors
      - Use of PWM and duty cycles
      - Applications in automation and robotics
    related_outcomes: [LO2, LO3]
  - id: T3
    title: Design Considerations for Timer-Based Systems
    content: Designing timer-based systems for proximity monitoring requires careful consideration of several factors, including sensor selection, system latency, and feedback mechanisms. Sensor selection is critical, as it influences the system's accuracy, range, and environmental adaptability. Latency must be minimized to ensure real-time responsiveness, particularly in applications like automated vehicles and industrial robots. Implementing feedback loops within the system architecture can provide dynamic adjustments to sensor parameters, enhancing stability and performance. Additionally, power consumption is a significant consideration, especially for battery-operated systems, where efficient power management can extend operational life without compromising functionality. Mathematical models play a crucial role in system design, allowing engineers to simulate and optimize performance before deployment. These models can encompass signal processing algorithms, control system dynamics, and predictive maintenance strategies, all of which contribute to a robust and reliable proximity monitoring solution. In smart home systems, these considerations ensure that devices like automated lighting and security systems operate efficiently and reliably.
    key_points:
      - Importance of sensor selection and system latency
      - Implementation of feedback loops
      - Power management strategies
      - Role of mathematical models in design
      - Real-world applications and challenges
    related_outcomes: [LO3, LO4]
  - id: T4
    title: Implementation and Testing of Timer-Based Systems
    content: The implementation of timer-based proximity monitoring systems involves both hardware and software components. Hardware implementation requires the integration of sensors, timers, microcontrollers, and communication interfaces. Software development includes writing code for timer configuration, sensor data processing, and control algorithms. Testing is a crucial phase that validates the system's functionality and performance under various conditions. This involves both simulation and real-world testing, where the system is subjected to different distances, speeds, and environmental factors to ensure robustness. Calibration and tuning of system parameters are often necessary to achieve desired performance levels. The iterative process of testing and refinement is essential to address any issues related to signal interference, timing inaccuracies, or sensor malfunctions. Industry standards and best practices guide the testing protocols, ensuring that the system meets safety and reliability requirements for its intended application. In the context of industrial automation, these systems must adhere to rigorous standards to ensure worker safety and operational efficiency.
    key_points:
      - Hardware and software integration
      - Importance of testing and validation
      - Calibration and tuning processes
      - Addressing real-world challenges
      - Industry standards and best practices
    related_outcomes: [LO1, LO4]
  - id: T5
    title: Mathematical Modeling of Timer-Based Systems
    content: Mathematical modeling is a cornerstone of designing efficient timer-based systems, providing a framework for analyzing and predicting system behavior. The fundamental equations governing timer operations include those for frequency, period, and duty cycle. For instance, the frequency $f$ of a timer is given by $f = \frac{1}{T}$, where $T$ is the period. The duty cycle, defined as the ratio of the active time to the total period, is crucial for applications requiring precise control over signal timing. In proximity monitoring systems, these models help in calculating the optimal timing for sensor activation and deactivation, ensuring that the system responds swiftly to changes in the environment. Additionally, the Laplace transform is often used to analyze system dynamics, allowing engineers to design controllers that stabilize the system and improve performance. In automotive radar systems, mathematical models are used to predict the distance and speed of nearby vehicles, enhancing adaptive cruise control features.
    key_points:
      - Importance of mathematical modeling in design
      - Equations for frequency, period, and duty cycle
      - Application of Laplace transforms in system analysis
      - Role in optimizing sensor timing
      - Use in automotive radar systems
    related_outcomes: [LO2, LO5]
  - id: T6
    title: Advanced Timer Configurations and Interrupt Handling
    content: Advanced timer configurations and interrupt handling are critical for enhancing the functionality of embedded systems. Timers can be configured in various modes, such as one-shot, periodic, and pulse-width modulation (PWM) modes, each serving different application needs. One-shot mode is used for generating a single event after a specified delay, while periodic mode is suitable for tasks requiring regular intervals. PWM mode is extensively used in motor control and LED dimming applications, where precise control over the output signal is necessary. Interrupts play a vital role in timer operations, allowing the system to respond immediately to time-critical events without polling. The configuration of interrupt priority and handling routines ensures that the system remains responsive and efficient. In the context of real-time operating systems (RTOS), timers and interrupts are used to manage task scheduling and resource allocation, enabling the development of complex, multitasking applications. For example, in drone flight control systems, timers and interrupts are used to manage sensor data acquisition and motor control, ensuring stable and responsive flight.
    key_points:
      - Various timer modes and their applications
      - Role of interrupts in enhancing system responsiveness
      - Configuration of interrupt priority and handling
      - Use in real-time operating systems
      - Applications in drone flight control
    related_outcomes: [LO3, LO5]

      - Timer resolution: 8 bits
    required: Timer counts for 75% duty cycle
    solution_steps:

        explanation: Calculate the total number of timer counts per cycle.

        explanation: Determine the active time in counts for a 75% duty cycle.
        calculation: $256 \times 0.75 = 192 \text{ counts}$
      - step: 3
        explanation: Calculate the inactive time in counts.
        calculation: $256 - 192 = 64 \text{ counts}$
      - step: 4
        explanation: Configure the timer to switch states after 192 counts.
        calculation: Set compare register to 192
      - step: 5
        explanation: Set the PWM output pin to high for the active period.
        calculation: Write PWM register settings
      - step: 6
        explanation: Configure the timer to reset after 256 counts.
        calculation: Set overflow register to 256
      - step: 7
        explanation: Verify PWM output using an oscilloscope.

        explanation: Adjust settings if the measured duty cycle is incorrect.

discussion_questions:
  - question: What are the advantages of using timer-based systems in embedded applications?
    type: comprehension
    related_outcome: LO1
    suggested_answer: Timer-based systems offer precise control over timing operations, reduce processor load by offloading timing tasks, and enhance system reliability by providing consistent time intervals.
  - question: How do proximity sensors differ in terms of technology and application?
    type: application
    related_outcome: LO2
    suggested_answer: Proximity sensors vary by technology, such as infrared, ultrasonic, and capacitive, each suited for different environments and applications, like detecting objects, measuring distance, or sensing material presence.
  - question: What factors must be considered when designing a timer-based proximity monitoring system?
    type: analysis
    related_outcome: LO3
    suggested_answer: Key factors include sensor range and accuracy, timer resolution, power consumption, environmental conditions, and system response time to ensure optimal performance and reliability.
  - question: Evaluate the impact of latency on the performance of proximity monitoring systems.
    type: evaluation
    related_outcome: LO3
    suggested_answer: Latency can significantly affect system responsiveness, leading to delayed actions or missed detections, which is critical in applications requiring real-time monitoring and quick response.
  - question: How can feedback loops enhance the functionality of timer-based systems?
    type: critical_thinking
    related_outcome: LO4
    suggested_answer: Feedback loops can improve system accuracy and stability by adjusting timing parameters based on real-time data, allowing for adaptive control and error correction in dynamic environments.
  - question: Describe the role of interrupts in timer-based proximity monitoring systems.
    type: recall
    related_outcome: LO1
    suggested_answer: Interrupts allow the system to respond immediately to specific events, such as sensor triggers, by pausing current tasks and executing predefined routines, enhancing system efficiency and responsiveness.
  - question: Compare the use of hardware timers versus software timers in embedded systems.
    type: analysis
    related_outcome: LO2
    suggested_answer: Hardware timers provide precise and reliable timing with minimal CPU intervention, while software timers offer flexibility and ease of implementation but may introduce variability due to processor load.
  - question: How does environmental noise affect the accuracy of proximity sensors, and what mitigation strategies can be employed?
    type: evaluation
    related_outcome: LO3
    suggested_answer: Environmental noise can cause false readings or reduced accuracy in proximity sensors. Mitigation strategies include using signal filtering, shielding, and selecting sensors with noise-resistant technologies.
  - question: Discuss the importance of calibration in the performance of a timer-based proximity monitoring system.
    type: comprehension
    related_outcome: LO5
    suggested_answer: Calibration ensures that sensors and timers operate within specified tolerances, improving accuracy and reliability by compensating for component variations and environmental changes.
  - question: Propose a method to test the reliability of a timer-based proximity monitoring system in a real-world scenario.
    type: critical_thinking
    related_outcome: LO4
    suggested_answer: A reliability test could involve subjecting the system to various environmental conditions and operational stresses, recording its performance metrics, and analyzing failure rates to identify potential improvements.

homework:
  title: "Timer-Based Proximity Monitoring System Homework"
  due_date: "Next class"
  problems:
    - id: HW1
      related_example: EX1
      question: Calculate the timer counts for a 10 ms interrupt on a microcontroller with a 10 MHz clock and a prescaler of 256. Provide the formula and steps used in your calculation.
      marks: 5
      model_answer: |
        To calculate the timer counts, use the formula:
        $$ \text{Timer Counts} = \frac{\text{Interrupt Time} \times \text{Clock Frequency}}{\text{Prescaler}} $$
        Substituting the given values:
        $$ \text{Timer Counts} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 10^6}{256} $$
        $$ \text{Timer Counts} = \frac{100,000}{256} $$
        $$ \text{Timer Counts} = 390.625 $$
        Therefore, the timer should be set to 391 counts (rounding up to the nearest whole number).
    - id: HW2
      related_example: EX2
      question: Design a PWM signal with a 60% duty cycle for a system with a 1 kHz frequency and 10-bit timer resolution. Explain the steps and calculations involved.
      marks: 10
      model_answer: |
        The PWM period is the inverse of the frequency:
        $$ \text{Period} = \frac{1}{\text{Frequency}} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1 \text{ ms} $$
        For a 60% duty cycle:
        $$ \text{On Time} = 0.6 \times 1 \text{ ms} = 0.6 \text{ ms} $$
        With a 10-bit resolution, the timer counts range from 0 to 1023. Calculate the counts for the on time:
        $$ \text{Counts} = \frac{\text{On Time}}{\text{Period}} \times 1023 $$
        $$ \text{Counts} = \frac{0.6}{1} \times 1023 = 613.8 $$
        Therefore, set the timer to 614 counts for the on time.
    - id: HW3
      related_example: EX3
      question: Optimize a timer-based system to achieve a response time of 8 ms for an infrared sensor with an initial latency of 25 ms. Describe the optimization process.
      marks: 10
      model_answer: |
        To optimize the response time, the system latency must be reduced by:
        $$ \text{Reduction Needed} = 25 \text{ ms} - 8 \text{ ms} = 17 \text{ ms} $$
        Possible strategies include:
        - Reducing the processing time by optimizing the code.
        - Increasing the clock speed, if possible.
        - Using a more efficient algorithm for sensor data processing.
        By implementing these strategies, the system can meet the 8 ms response time requirement.
    - id: HW4
      related_example: EX4
      question: Develop a multi-sensor integration strategy for a timer-based system with three sensors, each having a 15 ms response time, to achieve a total synchronization time of 20 ms. Outline your approach.
      marks: 15
      model_answer: |
        To achieve a total synchronization time of 20 ms:
        - Use parallel processing to handle sensor data simultaneously.
        - Implement a priority-based interrupt system to manage sensor data efficiently.
        - Optimize the data collection and processing algorithms to minimize delays.
        By coordinating sensor data collection and processing in parallel, the system can synchronize all three sensors within the 20 ms target.
